subcategory: The Source You Cannot Turn Off
questionText: With the DC disconnect open on a sunny day, the array-side conductors are:

choices.text: Still energized at full string Voc — only current flow stopped; light on the modules means live DC

explanation: The defining solar safety fact: sunrise energizes the generator regardless of switches. Isolation stops current, not voltage — plan all array-side work accordingly.

questionText: An inverter fails its wake-up isolation (Riso) test every dawn, then starts normally by late morning. The classic cause is:

choices.text: Moisture in a connector or junction box: dew-wet insulation leaks at dawn and dries as the sun climbs

explanation: The dawn-fault-noon-recovery pattern is the moisture signature. String-by-string megger isolation split-halves the array to the wet crimp.

questionText: An inverter input stage failed on the year's coldest clear dawn, months after a string was lengthened by two modules. The engineering check that was skipped is:

choices.text: Temperature-corrected string Voc against the inverter's maximum input at record-low design temperature

explanation: Voc peaks in cold. Strings sized against summer readings can cross the input ceiling at frigid dawn — the field's most consequential design rule.

questionText: An optimizer-equipped array reads zero volts at the inverter inputs everywhere. Before roof diagnosis, the RSD-literate check is:

choices.text: Whether rapid shutdown is initiated or its transmitter/power supply failed — obedient shutdown mimics total array failure

explanation: Module-level electronics collapse output without the keep-alive heartbeat. Zero-DC-everywhere may mean the safety system is in charge, not that the array died.

questionText: A grid-tie-only solar customer asks why their array produced nothing during a blackout. The answer is:

choices.text: Anti-islanding: certified inverters must stop exporting when the grid dies to protect lineworkers — backup requires batteries with islanding capability

explanation: Dead lines must stay dead for the crews working them. Standard grid-tie shuts down by design and law — the conversation that sells storage.

questionText: An inverter trips on AC overvoltage every sunny midday; your meter confirms 253 V at its terminals during export on a nominal-240 service. The professional path is:

choices.text: Widen the inverter's voltage window to stop the trips
Document the real site-voltage rise under export and escalate through the utility/interconnection channel — grid settings are agreement-governed

explanation: The inverter truthfully reports a soft grid rising under export. Settings implement the interconnection agreement — silencing trips removes protection where it just proved needed.

questionText: A 225 A busbar with a 200 A main breaker can accept a load-side solar backfeed breaker up to:

choices.text: 70 A, installed at the opposite end of the bus from the main

explanation: 225 × 1.2 = 270; 270 − 200 = 70 A — with opposite-end placement as part of the rule's physics.

questionText: A new battery charges from the grid at peak rates and discharges at midnight — exactly backwards from its time-of-use program. The classic error is:

choices.text: Reversed or misplaced consumption CTs — the system's power-direction senses are inverted

explanation: The CTs are the system's eyes on power flow; reversed CTs invert every decision. The most common storage commissioning error — found with a clamp meter and the live flow view.

questionText: A homeowner's battery has sat at 100% SOC for a month 'doing nothing.' Before suspecting hardware, check:

choices.text: The operating mode — a backup-reserve configuration holds full charge by design, awaiting an outage

explanation: Backup mode is supposed to look idle. Mode-vs-intent verification is the storage mis-config lesson before any hardware suspicion.

questionText: A wall battery shows visible case swelling. The correct field response is:

choices.text: Press it flat and reset the BMS
Do not charge, do not puncture — isolate per procedure and escalate; swelling is venting-pressure evidence of a compromised pack
Discharge it fully with a load

explanation: A swollen lithium module is the cell's own pre-runaway warning label. Quarantine and manufacturer procedures — never mechanical fixes.

questionText: Water is applied to a lithium BESS fire primarily to:

choices.text: Cool neighboring cells and stop propagation — the runaway chemistry self-oxidizes and cannot be smothered

explanation: Runaway supplies its own oxidizer; smothering fails and 'extinguished' packs reignite. Water's job is cascade-stopping cooling — and post-fire packs stay dangerous.

questionText: A string's IV trace shows expected voltage but a rounded, soft knee compared to its siblings. The signature indicates:

choices.text: Series-resistance problems: degraded connectors or cabling in that string's path

explanation: Resistance rounds the power point. The soft knee is the connector catalog's curve signature — IR imaging then finds the warm culprit.

questionText: Production fell 20% month-over-month but Performance Ratio held steady at 0.83. The finding is:

choices.text: Weather: the system converted available sun at unchanged efficiency — the sunnier month simply offered more

explanation: PR normalizes for measured irradiance: steady PR with lower totals is climatology. Falling PR is the real-problem signal.

questionText: Every string reads proportionally ~25% low on clear days with normal voltages and smooth curves. The suspect class is:

choices.text: Shared causes: uniform soiling, inverter thermal derating, or design clipping

explanation: Uniform proportional loss is the shared-cause signature: the common surface or the common electronics, never a single component.

questionText: A combiner string fuse is found open. Before re-fusing you must:

choices.text: Find the fault it protected against — megger and inspect the string; fuses open because parallel siblings backfed a fault

explanation: The universal fuse rule: protection that operated is a witness. The string carried current it should not have — find the fault the fuse survived.

questionText: Off-grid during an outage, an AC-coupled PV inverter cycles off repeatedly as the battery nears full. This is:

choices.text: The battery inverter frequency-shifting to throttle PV into a full battery — island power management by design

explanation: The grid-forming battery inverter speaks frequency-watt to its one-house grid: rising frequency tells PV to back off. Designed behavior that generates service calls.

questionText: Before separating an MC4 connector during daylight service you must:

choices.text: Verify the circuit carries zero current — voltage will be present regardless, but opening under load draws a sustained DC arc

explanation: Light = voltage, always; arcs need current. Confirm zero flow (inverter off, clamp-verified) before any connector opens — DC arcs do not self-extinguish.

questionText: Statistically, the solar trade's dominant injury mechanism is:

choices.text: Falls from roofs and ladders — harness and ladder discipline is the first safety religion

explanation: Gravity injures more solar workers than electricity. Fall protection discipline is what brings techs home.

questionText: A system passed AHJ inspection but the utility's Permission To Operate has not arrived. The system:

choices.text: Stays off/non-exporting until PTO — energizing early violates the interconnection agreement

explanation: PTO is the utility's separate gate. Early export breaches the agreement that makes the system legal and paid — the paperwork IS the project.

questionText: The solar industry's recognized certification body is:

choices.text: NABCEP — Associate credentials through the PV Installation Professional and specialist paths

explanation: NABCEP is solar's ladder — the analog of NATE, CFESA, and EGSA in the sibling trades, with Associate credentials as first-year targets.
